name: MD Thermochrome
classification_notes: Designed to evoke dot-matrix and thermal printer output, with optically corrected letterforms rather than strict pixel-grid constraints. The bitmap heritage and narrow, mechanical character place it firmly in display territory.
summary: A thermal and dot-matrix printer homage with eight weights and three slant variants, distinguished by optical corrections that lift it above pastiche.
description: Where most bitmap-revival typefaces reproduce the raw artefacts of dot-matrix or thermal printing, MD Thermochrome applies optical corrections and adjusted glyph shapes to achieve visual balance across its eight weights from Thin to Ultra. Each weight ships in five slope variants: Regular, Italic, Inclined, Reclined and Retalic, giving an unusually broad expressive range for a typeface with this kind of reference point. Released as a work in progress via Future Fonts, it includes extensive Latin Extended coverage alongside a large set of symbols, icons and currency glyphs.
foundry_blurb: Mass-Driver describe MD Thermochrome as a typeface designed to evoke dot-matrix and thermal printer fonts, but unlike the originals and other mimics, it features optical corrections and adjusted glyph shapes for better visual balance. It is available as a work in progress, with licensees receiving updates as the design develops.

name: MD Nichrome
classification_notes: Explicitly designed as a display typeface referencing the genre typography of 1970s and early 1980s sci-fi paperbacks, drawing on dry transfer and phototypesetting-era letterforms rather than any single historical revival.
summary: A display typeface distilling the genre typography of 1970s and early 1980s sci-fi paperbacks, drawing on multiple dry transfer and phototypesetting-era sources rather than reviving any single face.
description: Eight weights from Infra to Ultra, each with an oblique companion, delivered as a two-axis variable font covering weight and slant. The design synthesises details from numerous typefaces of the era that were originally produced for dry transfer or phototypesetting and never received a considered digital translation. Extended Latin coverage spans a very broad range of languages, making it practical well beyond purely decorative use.
foundry_blurb: Mass-Driver describe MD Nichrome as a display typeface referencing the typography found on sci-fi paperbacks from the 1970s and early 1980s. Many of the typefaces from that period and genre, originally drawn for dry transfer or phototypesetting, were never carefully digitised; MD Nichrome aims to remedy this for modern technologies and styles, not by reviving any single typeface but by capturing elements of many. It is available as a two-axis variable font with weight and slant axes.

name: MD Lórien
classification_notes: MD Lórien draws directly from late-17th to mid-18th-century Dutch Baroque typefaces, placing it firmly in the baroque serif tradition akin to the Caslon and Van Dijck lineage. The ornate historical source material is modernised for contemporary text use without abandoning its period character.
summary: A contemporary reworking of Dutch Baroque typefaces from the late 17th to mid 18th century, brought into a practical five-weight text family with full italic complements.
description: Drawing on a considered selection of Low Countries Baroque sources, MD Lórien adapts the ornate decorative qualities of that period into a family suited to demanding text work. Ten styles span Regular through Black, each with a matching italic, and the character set is extensive: small caps, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, ligatures, superscripts, subscripts and broad Latin language coverage are all included. The result sits between historical fidelity and contemporary utility in a way that makes it credible for long-form editorial and book work.
foundry_blurb: Mass-Driver describe MD Lórien as a new English interpretation of the Dutch Baroque, arriving roughly a quarter of a millennium after William Caslon's eponymous classic. Based on a careful selection of late-17th to mid-18th-century typefaces from the Low Countries, it gently adapts ornate, decorative Baroque designs into a practical contemporary family.

name: MD IO
classification_notes: MD IO is explicitly described as a monospaced typeface with fixed-width glyph construction, unconventional letterforms and functional ink traps optimised for screen and small-size legibility.
summary: A monospaced typeface with large functional ink traps and unconventional glyph constructions, optimised for legibility at small sizes on screen.
description: Eight weights from Thin to Ultra, each with a matching italic, give MD IO broad expressive range across interface and display contexts. Large ink traps, designed as legibility aids at small sizes, become distinctive stylistic features at larger settings. A two-axis variable font covering weight and italic is available on request with full family orders, and the face is designed to complement Mass-Driver's MD UI.
foundry_blurb: Mass-Driver describe MD IO as a contemporary monospaced typeface built for clarity and legibility at small sizes and on digital screens. Unconventional glyph constructions and large functional ink traps improve character recognition at text sizes, while those same features become eye-catching stylistic elements at display sizes. A two-axis variable font with weight and italic axes is available on request with full family purchases, and the typeface is designed to pair with MD UI.

name: MD System
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a workhorse grotesque with a rational family layout tempered by humanist warmth; the combination of systematic width variants, a mono subfamily and variable font axes places it firmly in the grotesque tradition.
summary: A four-subfamily grotesque from Mass-Driver spanning standard, narrow, condensed and mono widths, available as a three-axis variable font with weight, width and slant axes.
description: Built for general practice, MD System pairs a rational, grid-driven family structure with letterforms that retain traces of the human hand, resisting the blandness common to neutral grotesques. The family runs five weights from Regular to Black with italics across all four subfamilies, and the complete collection is offered as a three-axis variable font covering weight, width and slant. The mono subfamily ships separately as a two-axis variable font for weight and slant, making it practical for UI, editorial and coding contexts alike.
foundry_blurb: Mass-Driver describe MD System as a typeface for general practice: a workhorse grotesque combining a rational family layout with an appearance that embraces the human hand. Designed for versatility across a broad range of applications without resorting to blandness or neutrality, it is available as a three-axis variable font with weight, width and slant axes for the complete collection, or a two-axis variable font with weight and slant for the Mono family.

name: MD UI
classification_notes: MD UI is positioned explicitly within the International Typographic Style tradition, making it a neo-grotesque; humanist-influenced glyph construction in the XS cut adds warmth but the overall framework is firmly neo-grotesque.
summary: A neo-grotesque optimised across three optical cuts for everything from screen UI text to high-impact display work.
description: Three optical variants handle distinct jobs: the XS cut uses prominent ink traps, wide apertures and humanist glyph constructions to stay legible at small sizes and in degraded environments; the XL cut is tightly spaced and precision-drawn for display use; and the standard variant sits between them as an all-purpose workhorse. Each cut runs the full weight range from Thin to Ultra with matching italics, giving 48 styles in total. Designed as a companion to MD IO and rooted in the International Typographic Style.
foundry_blurb: Mass-Driver describe MD UI as the International Typographic Style projected one step into the future, a neo-grotesque tuned for maximum performance across all scales. The XS family features large ink traps, wide apertures and humanist-influenced constructions for legibility in challenging environments, the XL cut offers a tightly-spaced, high-impact display design, and the standard variant balances readability with style. It is designed to pair with MD IO.

name: MD Polychrome
classification_notes: Derived from MICR (Magnetic Ink Character Recognition) letterforms, a highly specialised pre-digital machine-readable script. Its optical-size structure and large-scale intent place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A four-style display typeface drawn from the aesthetic of Magnetic Ink Character Recognition type, refined for large-scale and poster use.
description: Taking MICR as its source, the design addresses the inherent unevenness of machine-readable letterforms: rebalancing texture, correcting spacing and pushing the optical range toward the largest print applications. Four styles named Nano, Kilo, Mega and Giga function as optical sizes, each tuned for different scales. A variable font is available on request with purchase of the complete family.
foundry_blurb: Mass-Driver describe MD Polychrome as a typeface based on the unique aesthetic of Magnetic Ink Character Recognition and the pre-digital typefaces it inspired. Available in four styles, it refines and expands the traditional MICR aesthetic by balancing its typically uneven texture, improving spacing and pushing the limits of optical size to produce a version fit for the largest posters and boldest layouts. A variable font is available on request with orders of the complete family.

name: MD Primer
classification_notes: MD Primer draws directly from late 19th-century European grotesque and American gothic type specimens, intentionally reproducing their mismatched proportions and idiosyncratic curves rather than rationalising them into a modern grotesque.
summary: A sans-serif that deliberately reconstructs the mismatched proportions and surplus curves of late 19th-century European grotesque and American gothic type, treating their inconsistencies as features rather than faults.
description: Drawing on a documented range of historical specimens from foundries including Bauer'sche Gießerei, Schelter & Giesecke and Marder, Luse & Co., MD Primer treats the irregularities of pre-standardisation sans-serif type as its defining characteristic. Six weights from Light to Black each come with matching italics, giving the family a working range beyond its display origins. The extended Latin character set covers a broad sweep of languages, making it more practical than its historical premise might suggest.
foundry_blurb: Mass-Driver describe MD Primer as a digital reinterpretation of the flaws and inconsistencies found in early sans-serifs, inspired by European grotesque and American gothic designs from the late 19th century. They frame its mismatched proportions and surplus curves not as defects to be corrected but as qualities to be celebrated, positioning the typeface as a tribute to the unrefined character of pre-modern type design.
